**Abraham Lincoln**[[b]](#cite_note-4) (February 12, 1809 – April 15, 1865) was the 16th [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), serving from 1861 until [his assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Assassination of Abraham Lincoln") in 1865. He led the United States through the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), defeating the [Confederate States](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") and playing a major role in the [abolition of slavery](/wiki/End_of_slavery_in_the_United_States "End of slavery in the United States").

Born in [a one-room log cabin](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_Birthplace_National_Historical_Park "Abraham Lincoln Birthplace National Historical Park") in Kentucky, Lincoln was raised on the [frontier](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier"). He was [self-educated](/wiki/Autodidacticism "Autodidacticism") and became a lawyer, [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") state [legislator](/wiki/Illinois_House_of_Representatives "Illinois House of Representatives"), and [U.S. representative](/wiki/U.S._representative "U.S. representative"). Angered by the [Kansas–Nebraska Act](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act") of 1854, which opened the territories to slavery, he became a leader of the new [Republican Party](/wiki/History_of_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "History of the Republican Party (United States)"). He reached a national audience in the [1858 Senate campaign debates](/wiki/Lincoln%E2%80%93Douglas_debates "Lincoln–Douglas debates") against [Stephen A. Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas"). Lincoln won the [1860 presidential election](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election"), becoming the first Republican president. His victory prompted a majority of the slave states to begin to [secede](/wiki/Secession_in_the_United_States "Secession in the United States") and form the Confederate States. A month after Lincoln assumed the presidency, Confederate forces [attacked Fort Sumter](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Sumter "Battle of Fort Sumter"), starting the Civil War.

As a [moderate Republican](/wiki/Moderate_Republicans_(Reconstruction_era) "Moderate Republicans (Reconstruction era)"), Lincoln had to navigate conflicting political opinions from contentious factions during the war effort. He closely supervised the Union's strategy and tactics, including the selection of generals and the implementation of a [naval blockade](/wiki/Union_blockade "Union blockade") of Southern ports. He suspended the writ of *[habeas corpus](/wiki/Habeas_corpus "Habeas corpus")* in April 1861, an action that Chief Justice [Roger Taney](/wiki/Roger_Taney "Roger Taney") found in *[Ex parte Merryman](/wiki/Ex_parte_Merryman "Ex parte Merryman")* that only Congress could do, and he averted war with [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") by defusing the [*Trent* Affair](/wiki/Trent_Affair "Trent Affair"). On January 1, 1863, he issued the [Emancipation Proclamation](/wiki/Emancipation_Proclamation "Emancipation Proclamation"), which declared the slaves in the states "in rebellion" to be free. On November 19, 1863, he delivered the [Gettysburg Address](/wiki/Gettysburg_Address "Gettysburg Address"), which became one of the most famous speeches in American history. He promoted the [Thirteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Thirteenth_Amendment_to_the_U.S._Constitution "Thirteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution"), which, in 1865, abolished [chattel slavery](/wiki/Chattel_slavery "Chattel slavery"). Following his [re-election in 1864](/wiki/1864_United_States_presidential_election "1864 United States presidential election"), he sought to heal the war-torn nation through [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era "Reconstruction era").

On April 14, 1865, five days after the [Confederate surrender at Appomattox](/wiki/Battle_of_Appomattox_Court_House "Battle of Appomattox Court House"), Lincoln was attending a play at [Ford's Theatre](/wiki/Ford%27s_Theatre "Ford's Theatre") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington_County,_D.C. "Washington County, D.C."), when he was fatally shot by [John Wilkes Booth](/wiki/John_Wilkes_Booth "John Wilkes Booth"), a Confederate sympathizer. Lincoln is remembered as a [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") and a national hero for his wartime leadership and for his efforts to preserve the [Union](/wiki/Perpetual_Union "Perpetual Union") and abolish slavery. [He is consistently ranked](/wiki/Historical_rankings_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#Scholar_survey_summary "Historical rankings of presidents of the United States") in both popular and scholarly polls as among the greatest presidents in [American history](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States "History of the United States").

## Family and childhood

### Early life

Main article: [Early life and career of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Early_life_and_career_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Early life and career of Abraham Lincoln")

Born in [a one-room log cabin](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_Birthplace_National_Historical_Park "Abraham Lincoln Birthplace National Historical Park") in Kentucky on February 12, 1809, Abraham Lincoln was raised on [Sinking Spring Farm](/wiki/Sinking_Spring_Farm "Sinking Spring Farm") near [Hodgenville, Kentucky](/wiki/Hodgenville,_Kentucky "Hodgenville, Kentucky").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199620–22-5)[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1) The second child of [Thomas Lincoln](/wiki/Thomas_Lincoln "Thomas Lincoln") and [Nancy Hanks Lincoln](/wiki/Nancy_Hanks_Lincoln "Nancy Hanks Lincoln"), he was a descendant of [Samuel Lincoln](/wiki/Samuel_Lincoln "Samuel Lincoln"), an Englishman who migrated to [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") in 1638,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._1–4-6) and of the [Harrison family of Virginia](/wiki/Harrison_family_of_Virginia "Harrison family of Virginia").[[c]](#cite_note-8) His paternal grandfather and namesake, [Captain Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Captain_Abraham_Lincoln "Captain Abraham Lincoln"), moved the family from [Virginia](/wiki/Colony_of_Virginia "Colony of Virginia") to Kentucky. The captain was killed in [a Native American raid](/wiki/Northwest_Indian_War "Northwest Indian War") in 1786.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._1–4-6) The family settled in [Hardin County, Kentucky](/wiki/Hardin_County,_Kentucky "Hardin County, Kentucky"), in the early 1800s.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199621–22-9) Nancy is widely assumed to have been the daughter of Lucy Hanks.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartelt200879-10) Thomas and Nancy married on June 12, 1806, and moved to [Elizabethtown, Kentucky](/wiki/Elizabethtown,_Kentucky "Elizabethtown, Kentucky").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._4-11) They had three children: Sarah, Abraham, and Thomas; Thomas died as an infant.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite200918-12)

Lincoln's father bought multiple farms in Kentucky but could not get clear [property titles](/wiki/Recording_(real_estate) "Recording (real estate)") to any, losing hundreds of acres in legal disputes.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199622–24Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._6–8-13) In 1816, the family moved to [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), where land titles were more reliable.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinkle20116–7-14) They settled on a forested plot in [Little Pigeon Creek Community](/wiki/Little_Pigeon_Creek_Community "Little Pigeon Creek Community").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._22–23-15) In Kentucky and Indiana, Thomas worked as a farmer, cabinetmaker, and carpenter.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartelt200834,_156-16) At various times, he owned farms, livestock, and town lots, appraised estates, and served on county patrols. Thomas and Nancy were members of a [Separate Baptist Church](/wiki/Separate_Baptists "Separate Baptists"), a pious evangelical group whose members largely opposed slavery.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199624-17) Overcoming financial challenges, Thomas obtained [clear title](/wiki/Clear_title "Clear title") to 80 acres (32 ha) in Little Pigeon Creek Community in 1827.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartelt200824,_104-18)

On October 5, 1818, Nancy died from [milk sickness](/wiki/Milk_sickness "Milk sickness"), leaving 11-year-old Sarah in charge of the household, which included her father, 9-year-old Abraham, and Nancy's 19-year-old orphan cousin, Dennis Hanks.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartelt200822–23,_77-19) Thomas married [Sarah Bush Johnston](/wiki/Sarah_Bush_Johnston "Sarah Bush Johnston"), a widow with three children of her own, on December 2, 1819.[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1) Abraham became close to his stepmother and called her "Mama".[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199627–28-20) On January 20, 1828, Lincoln's sister died in childbirth, devastating him.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199634,_116-21)

### Education and move to Illinois

Lincoln was largely self-educated.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartelt200810,_33-22) His formal schooling was from [itinerant teachers](/wiki/Itinerant_teacher "Itinerant teacher"). It included two short stints in Kentucky, where he learned to read, but probably not to write. After moving to Indiana at age seven, he attended school only sporadically, for a total of less than 12 months by age 15.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199623,_29-23) Nevertheless, he was an avid reader and retained a lifelong interest in learning.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMadison2014110-24)

When Lincoln was a teenager his father relied heavily on him for farmwork and for supplementary income, hiring the boy out to area farmers and pocketing the money, as was the custom at the time.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._30,_41-25) When he was somewhat older, Lincoln and some friends took a job carrying goods by [flatboat](/wiki/Flatboat "Flatboat") to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), Louisiana, where the [slave markets](/wiki/Slave_market "Slave market"), according to the historian [Michael Burlingame](/wiki/Michael_Burlingame "Michael Burlingame"), "would leave an indelible impression on him.... It was the first time, but not the last, that he would be repelled while observing slavery firsthand."[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._44-26)

In March 1830, fearing another milk-sickness outbreak, several members of the extended Lincoln family, including Abraham, moved west to Illinois and settled in [Macon County](/wiki/Macon_County,_Illinois "Macon County, Illinois").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199636-27) Abraham became increasingly distant from Thomas, in part due to his father's lack of interest in education;[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBartelt200871-28) he would later refuse to attend his father's deathbed or funeral in 1851.[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1)

### Marriage and children

Further information: [Lincoln family](/wiki/Lincoln_family "Lincoln family"), [Health of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Health_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Health of Abraham Lincoln"), and [Sexuality of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Sexuality_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Sexuality of Abraham Lincoln")

[![Black-and-white photo of a woman with two young boys](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Mary%2C_Willie%2C_and_Tad_Lincoln%2C_c1860.jpg/250px-Mary%2C_Willie%2C_and_Tad_Lincoln%2C_c1860.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mary,_Willie,_and_Tad_Lincoln,_c1860.jpg)

Mary Todd Lincoln with Willie and Tad

Some historians, such as Michael Burlingame, identify Lincoln's first romantic interest as [Ann Rutledge](/wiki/Ann_Rutledge "Ann Rutledge"), a young woman also from Kentucky whom he met when he moved to [New Salem, Illinois](/wiki/Lincoln%27s_New_Salem "Lincoln's New Salem").[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._98-29) [Lewis Gannett](/wiki/Lewis_Gannett "Lewis Gannett") disputes that the evidence supports a romantic relationship between the two.[[27]](#cite_note-30) [David Herbert Donald](/wiki/David_Herbert_Donald "David Herbert Donald") states that "How that friendship [between Lincoln and Rutledge] developed into a romance cannot be reconstructed from the record".[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199656–57-31) Rutledge died on August 25, 1835, of [typhoid fever](/wiki/Typhoid_fever "Typhoid fever"). Lincoln took her death very hard, sinking into a serious depression and contemplating suicide.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199655–58Burlingame2008vol._1_p._100-32)[[30]](#cite_note-Atlanticoct2005-33)

In the early 1830s, Lincoln met [Mary Owens](/wiki/Mary_Owens_(Abraham_Lincoln_fianc%C3%A9e) "Mary Owens (Abraham Lincoln fiancée)") from Kentucky.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas200856–57,_69–70-34) Late in 1836, Lincoln agreed to a match with Owens if she returned to New Salem. Owens arrived that November and he courted her, but they both had second thoughts. On August 16, 1837, he wrote Owens a letter saying he would not blame her if she ended the relationship, and she declined to marry him.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199667–69-35)

In 1839, Lincoln met [Mary Todd](/wiki/Mary_Todd_Lincoln "Mary Todd Lincoln") in [Springfield, Illinois](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois"), and the following year they became engaged.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199680–86Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._174–176-36) She was a daughter of [Robert Smith Todd](/wiki/Robert_Smith_Todd "Robert Smith Todd"), a wealthy lawyer and businessman in [Lexington, Kentucky](/wiki/Lexington,_Kentucky "Lexington, Kentucky").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELambSwain20083-37) Lincoln initially broke off the engagement in early 1841, but the two were reconciled and married on November 4, 1842.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._182,_194–195-38) In 1844, the couple bought [a house](/wiki/Lincoln_Home_National_Historic_Site "Lincoln Home National Historic Site") in Springfield near his law office.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaker1989142-39) The marriage was turbulent; Mary was verbally abusive and at times physically violent towards her husband.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._202-40)

They had four sons. The eldest, [Robert Todd Lincoln](/wiki/Robert_Todd_Lincoln "Robert Todd Lincoln"), was born in 1843, and was the only child to live to maturity. [Edward Baker "Eddie" Lincoln](/wiki/Edward_Baker_Lincoln "Edward Baker Lincoln"), born in 1846, died February 1, 1850, probably of [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"). Lincoln's third son, [William Wallace "Willie" Lincoln](/wiki/William_Wallace_Lincoln "William Wallace Lincoln"), was born on December 21, 1850, and died of a fever at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") on February 20, 1862. The youngest, [Thomas "Tad" Lincoln](/wiki/Tad_Lincoln "Tad Lincoln"), was born on April 4, 1853, and died of [edema](/wiki/Edema "Edema") at age 18 on July 16, 1871.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009179–181,_476Manning201624,_34,_58,_73,_75,_139,_181-41) Lincoln loved children,[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009126-42) and the Lincolns were not considered to be strict with their own.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaker1989120Manning201639,_42-43) The deaths of Eddie and Willie had profound effects on both parents. Lincoln suffered from "[melancholy](/wiki/History_of_depression "History of depression")", a condition now thought to be [clinical depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder").[[30]](#cite_note-Atlanticoct2005-33)

### Early vocations and militia service

Further information: [Early life and career of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Early_life_and_career_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Early life and career of Abraham Lincoln") and [Abraham Lincoln in the Black Hawk War](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_in_the_Black_Hawk_War "Abraham Lincoln in the Black Hawk War")

In 1831, Lincoln's father moved the family to [a new homestead](/wiki/Lincoln_Log_Cabin_State_Historic_Site "Lincoln Log Cabin State Historic Site") in [Coles County, Illinois](/wiki/Coles_County,_Illinois "Coles County, Illinois"), after which Abraham struck out on his own.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEManning201612-44) He made his home in [New Salem, Illinois](/wiki/Lincoln%27s_New_Salem "Lincoln's New Salem"), for six years.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas200823–53-45) During 1831 and 1832, Lincoln worked at a general store in New Salem.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinkle200186–95-46) He gained a reputation for strength and courage after winning a [wrestling](/wiki/Scholastic_wrestling "Scholastic wrestling") match with the leader of a group of ruffians known as the Clary's Grove boys.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199640–41-47) In 1832, he declared his candidacy for the [Illinois House of Representatives](/wiki/Illinois_House_of_Representatives "Illinois House of Representatives"), though he interrupted his campaign to serve as a captain in the [Illinois Militia](/wiki/Illinois_Militia "Illinois Militia") during the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War").[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinkle200186–95-46) He was elected the captain of his militia company but did not see combat.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._67-48) In his political campaigning, Lincoln advocated for navigational improvements on the [Sangamon River](/wiki/Sangamon_River "Sangamon River").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._72-49) He drew crowds as a [raconteur](/wiki/Raconteur "Raconteur"), but he lacked name recognition, powerful friends, and money, and he lost the election.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWinkle2001114–116Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._71–75-50)

When Lincoln returned home from the war, he planned to become a [blacksmith](/wiki/Blacksmith "Blacksmith") but instead purchased a New Salem general store in partnership with William Berry. Because a license was required to sell customers alcoholic beverages, Berry obtained bartending licenses for Lincoln and himself, and in 1833 the [Lincoln–Berry General Store](/wiki/Lincoln-Berry_General_Store "Lincoln-Berry General Store") became a [tavern](/wiki/Tavern "Tavern") as well.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeacham202238-51) Burlingame wrote that Berry was "an undisciplined, hard-drinking fellow", and Lincoln "was too soft-hearted to deny anyone credit".[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._76-52) Although the economy was booming, the business struggled and went into debt, prompting Lincoln to sell his share.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeacham202238-51)

Lincoln served as New Salem's [postmaster](/wiki/Postmaster "Postmaster") and later as [county surveyor](/wiki/County_surveyor "County surveyor"), but he continued his voracious reading and decided to become a lawyer.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._77–81,_87-53) Rather than studying in the office of an established attorney, as was customary, Lincoln [read law](/wiki/Reading_law "Reading law") on his own, borrowing legal texts, including [Blackstone](/wiki/William_Blackstone "William Blackstone")'s *[Commentaries](/wiki/Commentaries_on_the_Laws_of_England "Commentaries on the Laws of England")* and [Chitty](/wiki/Joseph_Chitty "Joseph Chitty")'s *Pleadings*, from attorney [John Todd Stuart](/wiki/John_Todd_Stuart "John Todd Stuart").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKent2022121–122-54) He later said of his legal education that he "studied with nobody."[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199655-55)

## Early political offices and prairie lawyer

### Illinois state legislature (1834–1842)

[![Front view of a historic home](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Abes_House.JPG/250px-Abes_House.JPG)](/wiki/File:Abes_House.JPG)

[Lincoln's home](/wiki/Lincoln_Home_National_Historic_Site "Lincoln Home National Historic Site") in [Springfield, Illinois](/wiki/Springfield,_Illinois "Springfield, Illinois"), where he resided from 1844 until becoming president in 1861

In Lincoln's second state house campaign in 1834, as a supporter of [Whig Party](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") leader [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay"), he finished second among thirteen candidates running for four places.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite200959-56) Lincoln echoed Clay's support for the [American Colonization Society](/wiki/American_Colonization_Society "American Colonization Society"), which advocated [abolition](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") in conjunction with settling freed slaves in [Liberia](/wiki/Liberia "Liberia").[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFoner201017–19,_67-57) The Whigs also favored economic modernization in banking, tariffs to fund [internal improvements](/wiki/Internal_improvements "Internal improvements") such as railroads, and urbanization.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBorittPinsker2002137–153-58)

Lincoln served four terms in the Illinois House of Representatives for [Sangamon County](/wiki/Sangamon_County "Sangamon County").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimon1990283-59) In this role, he championed construction of the [Illinois and Michigan Canal](/wiki/Illinois_and_Michigan_Canal "Illinois and Michigan Canal").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._102-60) Lincoln voted to expand suffrage beyond White landowners to all White men.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimon1990130-61) He supported the chartering of the Illinois State Bank,[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarwardine200316-62) and also led a successful campaign for moving the state capital from [Vandalia](/wiki/Vandalia,_Illinois "Vandalia, Illinois") to Springfield.[[60]](#cite_note-63)

On January 27, 1838, Lincoln delivered [an address at the Lyceum](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln%27s_Lyceum_address "Abraham Lincoln's Lyceum address") in Springfield, after the murder of the anti-slavery newspaper editor [Elijah Parish Lovejoy](/wiki/Elijah_Parish_Lovejoy "Elijah Parish Lovejoy"). In this ostensibly non-partisan speech, Lincoln attacked [Stephen A. Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas") and the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), who the Whigs argued were supporting "mobocracy". His speech also attacked anti-abolitionism and racial bigotry.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._140–142-64) He was criticized in the press for a planned [duel](/wiki/Duel#United_States_2 "Duel") with [James Shields](/wiki/James_Shields_(politician,_born_1806) "James Shields (politician, born 1806)"), whom he had ridiculed in letters published under the name "Aunt Rebecca". Although the duel ultimately did not take place, Burlingame noted that "the affair embarrassed Lincoln terribly".[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._190–194-65)

### U.S. House of Representatives (1847–1849)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Abraham_Lincoln_by_Nicholas_Shepherd%2C_1846-crop.jpg/250px-Abraham_Lincoln_by_Nicholas_Shepherd%2C_1846-crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Lincoln_by_Nicholas_Shepherd,_1846-crop.jpg)

Daguerreotype of Lincoln, taken around 1847, the earliest known photograph of him[[63]](#cite_note-66)

In 1843, Lincoln sought the Whig nomination for [Illinois's 7th district seat](/wiki/Illinois%27s_7th_congressional_district "Illinois's 7th congressional district") in the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"); [John J. Hardin](/wiki/John_J._Hardin "John J. Hardin") was the winning candidate, though Lincoln convinced the party convention to limit Hardin to one term.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._215–218-67) Lincoln not only gained the nomination in 1846, but also won the election.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._231–241-68) The only Whig in the Illinois delegation, he was assigned to the [Committee on Post Office and Post Roads](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Post_Office_and_Post_Roads "United States House Committee on Post Office and Post Roads") and the [Committee on Expenditures in the War Department](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Expenditures_in_the_War_Department "United States House Committee on Expenditures in the War Department").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._263–265-69) Lincoln teamed with [Joshua R. Giddings](/wiki/Joshua_R._Giddings "Joshua R. Giddings") on a bill to abolish slavery in the [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia_(until_1871) "District of Columbia (until 1871)"), but dropped the bill when it failed to attract support from most other Whigs.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris200754Foner201057-70)[[68]](#cite_note-71)

Lincoln spoke against the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") (1846–1848), for which he said President [James K. Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk") had "some strong motive ... to involve the two countries in a war, and trusting to escape scrutiny, by fixing the public gaze upon the exceeding brightness of military glory—that attractive rainbow, that rises in showers of blood".[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeidlerHeidler2006181–183Greenberg2012250–251-72) He supported the [Wilmot Proviso](/wiki/Wilmot_Proviso "Wilmot Proviso"), a failed 1846 proposal to ban slavery in any U.S. territory won from Mexico.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolzer200463-73) Polk insisted that Mexican soldiers had begun the war by "invading the territory of the State of Texas ... and shedding the blood of our citizens on our own soil".[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._265-74) In his 1847 "[spot resolutions](/wiki/Spot_resolutions "Spot resolutions")", Lincoln rhetorically demanded that Polk tell Congress the exact "spot" where this occurred, but the Polk administration did not respond.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996123–124-75)[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1) His approach and rhetoric cost Lincoln political support in his district, and newspapers derisively nicknamed him "spotty Lincoln".[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1)

Lincoln had pledged in 1846 to serve only one term in the House.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996124-76) At the [1848 Whig National Convention](/wiki/1848_Whig_National_Convention "1848 Whig National Convention"), he initially supported Henry Clay and was among the leaders of the Illinois delegation backing Clay, voting for him on the early ballots. When it became clear that Clay could not secure the nomination, Lincoln joined other Whigs in shifting support to [Zachary Taylor](/wiki/Zachary_Taylor "Zachary Taylor"), who won the party’s nomination and the presidency in 1848. [[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996126–127-77) After Taylor won, Lincoln hoped in vain to be appointed commissioner of the [United States General Land Office](/wiki/United_States_General_Land_Office "United States General Land Office").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996138–140Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._296–303-78) The administration offered to appoint him secretary of the [Oregon Territory](/wiki/Oregon_Territory "Oregon Territory") instead.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._305–307-79) This would have disrupted his legal and political career in Illinois, so he declined and resumed his law practice.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris200755–57-80)

### Prairie lawyer

See also: [List of cases involving Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/List_of_cases_involving_Abraham_Lincoln "List of cases involving Abraham Lincoln")

Lincoln was [admitted to the Illinois bar](/wiki/Admission_to_the_bar_in_the_United_States "Admission to the bar in the United States") on September 9, 1836.[[78]](#cite_note-81) He moved to Springfield and began to practice law under [John T. Stuart](/wiki/John_T._Stuart "John T. Stuart"), Mary Todd's cousin.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite200971,_79,_108-82) He partnered for several years with [Stephen T. Logan](/wiki/Stephen_T._Logan "Stephen T. Logan") and, in 1844, began [his practice](/wiki/Lincoln-Herndon_Law_Offices_State_Historic_Site "Lincoln-Herndon Law Offices State Historic Site") with [William Herndon](/wiki/William_Herndon_(lawyer) "William Herndon (lawyer)").[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199688–101-83)

In his Springfield practice, according to Donald, Lincoln handled "virtually every kind of business that could come before a prairie lawyer".[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald199696-84) He dealt with many transportation cases in the midst of the nation's western expansion, particularly river barge conflicts under the new railroad bridges. In 1849 he received [a patent for a flotation device](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln%27s_patent "Abraham Lincoln's patent") for the movement of [riverboats](/wiki/Riverboat "Riverboat") in shallow water[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmerson200927–28-85) and Lincoln initially favored riverboat legal interests, but he represented whoever hired him.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996156–157Burlingame2008vol._1_p._253-86) He represented a bridge company against a riverboat company in *[Hurd v. Rock Island Bridge Company](/wiki/Hurd_v._Rock_Island_Bridge_Company "Hurd v. Rock Island Bridge Company")*, a landmark case involving a canal boat that sank after hitting a bridge.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGinty2015-87) His patent was never commercialized, but it made Lincoln the only president to hold a patent.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEmerson200927–28-85) Lincoln appeared before the [Illinois Supreme Court](/wiki/Illinois_Supreme_Court "Illinois Supreme Court") in 411 cases.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._186-88) From 1853 to 1860, one of his largest clients was the [Illinois Central Railroad](/wiki/Illinois_Central_Railroad "Illinois Central Railroad"), which Lincoln successfully sued to recover his legal fees.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996155–156,_196–197-89)

Lincoln represented William "Duff" Armstrong in his 1858 trial for the murder of James Preston Metzker.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996150–151-90) The case is famous for Lincoln's use of a fact established by [judicial notice](/wiki/Judicial_notice "Judicial notice") to challenge the credibility of an eyewitness. After a witness testified to seeing the crime in the moonlight, Lincoln produced a *[Farmers' Almanac](/wiki/Farmers%27_Almanac "Farmers' Almanac")* showing the Moon was at a low angle, drastically reducing visibility. Armstrong was acquitted.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._345–346-91) In an 1859 murder case, he defended "Peachy" Quinn Harrison, the grandson of [Peter Cartwright](/wiki/Peter_Cartwright_(revivalist) "Peter Cartwright (revivalist)"), Lincoln's political opponent.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._560-92) Harrison was charged with the murder of Greek Crafton who, according to Cartwright, said as he lay dying that he had "brought it upon myself" and that he forgave Harrison.[[90]](#cite_note-93) Lincoln angrily protested the judge's initial decision to exclude Cartwright's claim as [hearsay](/wiki/Hearsay "Hearsay"). Lincoln argued that the testimony involved a [dying declaration](/wiki/Dying_declaration "Dying declaration") and so was not subject to the hearsay rule. Instead of holding Lincoln in [contempt of court](/wiki/Contempt_of_court#United_States "Contempt of court") as expected, the judge, a Democrat, admitted the testimony into evidence, resulting in Harrison's acquittal.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996150–151Burlingame2008vol._1_p._560-94)

## Republican politics (1854–1860)

Main article: [Political career of Abraham Lincoln (1849–1861)](/wiki/Political_career_of_Abraham_Lincoln_(1849%E2%80%931861) "Political career of Abraham Lincoln (1849–1861)")

### Emergence as Republican leader

Further information: [Slave states and free states](/wiki/Slave_states_and_free_states "Slave states and free states") and [Abraham Lincoln and slavery](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln_and_slavery "Abraham Lincoln and slavery")

[![Head and shoulders portrait of a clean-shaven Lincoln](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Abraham_Lincoln_by_Byers%2C_1858_-_crop.jpg/250px-Abraham_Lincoln_by_Byers%2C_1858_-_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Lincoln_by_Byers,_1858_-_crop.jpg)

Lincoln in 1858, the year of [his debates](/wiki/Lincoln%E2%80%93Douglas_debates "Lincoln–Douglas debates") with [Stephen Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas") over slavery

The [Compromise of 1850](/wiki/Compromise_of_1850 "Compromise of 1850") failed to alleviate tensions over slavery between the slave-holding South and the free North.[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009175–176-95) As the slavery debate in the [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska_Territory "Nebraska Territory") and [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_Territory "Kansas Territory") territories became particularly acrimonious, Illinois Senator Stephen A. Douglas proposed [popular sovereignty](/wiki/Popular_sovereignty_in_the_United_States#Emergence_of_the_term_"popular_sovereignty"_and_its_pejorative_connotation "Popular sovereignty in the United States") as a compromise; the measure would allow the electorate of each territory to decide the status of slavery. The legislation alarmed many Northerners, who sought to prevent the spread of slavery, but Douglas's [Kansas–Nebraska Act](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act") narrowly passed Congress in May 1854.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009188–190-96) Lincoln's [Peoria Speech](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln%27s_Peoria_speech "Abraham Lincoln's Peoria speech") of October 1854, in which he declared his opposition to slavery,[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThomas2008148–152-97) was one of an estimated 175 speeches he delivered in the next six years on the topic of excluding slavery from the territories.[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1) Lincoln's attacks on the Kansas–Nebraska Act marked his return to political life.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009203–205-98)

Nationally, the Whigs were irreparably split by the Kansas–Nebraska Act and other ineffective efforts to compromise on the slavery issue. Reflecting on the demise of his party, Lincoln wrote in 1855, "I think I am a whig; but others say there are no whigs, and that I am an abolitionist.... I now do no more than oppose the *extension* of slavery."[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009215-99) The new [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") was formed as a northern party dedicated to anti-slavery, drawing from the anti-slavery wing of the [Whig](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") Party and combining [Free Soil](/wiki/Free_Soil_Party "Free Soil Party"), [Liberty](/wiki/Liberty_Party_(United_States,_1840) "Liberty Party (United States, 1840)"), and anti-slavery Democratic Party members,[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGovern200938–39-100) Lincoln resisted early Republican entreaties, fearing that the new party would become a platform for extreme abolitionists.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009203–204-101) Lincoln held out hope for rejuvenating the Whigs, though he lamented his party's growing closeness with the nativist [Know Nothing](/wiki/Know_Nothing "Know Nothing") movement.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009191–194-102) In 1854, Lincoln was elected to the Illinois legislature, but before the term began he declined to take his seat so that he would be eligible to run in the upcoming U.S. Senate election.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009203–205Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._390,_392-103) At that time, senators were elected by state legislatures. After leading in the first six rounds of voting, Lincoln was unable to obtain a majority. Lincoln instructed his backers to vote for [Lyman Trumbull](/wiki/Lyman_Trumbull "Lyman Trumbull"), an anti-slavery Democrat who had received few votes in the earlier ballots. Lincoln's decision to withdraw enabled his Whig supporters and Trumbull's anti-slavery Democrats to combine and defeat the mainstream Democratic candidate, [Joel Aldrich Matteson](/wiki/Joel_Aldrich_Matteson "Joel Aldrich Matteson").[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009205–208-104)

#### 1856 campaign

[Violent political confrontations in Kansas](/wiki/Bleeding_Kansas "Bleeding Kansas") continued, and opposition to the Kansas–Nebraska Act remained strong throughout the North. As the [1856 elections](/wiki/1856_United_States_elections "1856 United States elections") approached, Lincoln joined the Republicans and attended the [Bloomington Convention](/wiki/Bloomington_Convention "Bloomington Convention"), where the [Illinois Republican Party](/wiki/Illinois_Republican_Party "Illinois Republican Party") was established. The convention platform endorsed Congress's right to regulate slavery in the territories and backed the admission of Kansas as a free state. Lincoln gave the [final speech](/wiki/Lincoln%27s_Lost_Speech "Lincoln's Lost Speech") of the convention, calling for the preservation of the Union.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009216–221-105) At the June [1856 Republican National Convention](/wiki/1856_Republican_National_Convention "1856 Republican National Convention"), Lincoln received support to run as vice president, but ultimately the party put forward a ticket of [John C. Frémont](/wiki/John_C._Fr%C3%A9mont "John C. Frémont") and [William Dayton](/wiki/William_Dayton "William Dayton"), which Lincoln supported throughout Illinois. The Democrats nominated [James Buchanan](/wiki/James_Buchanan "James Buchanan") and the Know Nothings nominated [Millard Fillmore](/wiki/Millard_Fillmore "Millard Fillmore").[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009224–228-106) Buchanan prevailed, while Republican [William Henry Bissell](/wiki/William_Henry_Bissell "William Henry Bissell") won election as Governor of Illinois, and Lincoln became a leading Republican in Illinois.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009229–230-107)[[d]](#cite_note-109)

#### *Dred Scott v. Sandford*

Main article: [*Dred Scott v. Sandford*](/wiki/Dred_Scott_v._Sandford "Dred Scott v. Sandford")

[Dred Scott](/wiki/Dred_Scott "Dred Scott") was a slave whose master took him from a slave state to a territory that was free as a result of the [Missouri Compromise](/wiki/Missouri_Compromise "Missouri Compromise"). After Scott was returned to the slave state, he petitioned a federal court for his freedom. His petition was denied in *[Dred Scott v. Sandford](/wiki/Dred_Scott_v._Sandford "Dred Scott v. Sandford")* (1857).[[106]](#cite_note-110) Supreme Court Chief Justice [Roger B. Taney](/wiki/Roger_B._Taney "Roger B. Taney") wrote in his opinion that the Missouri Compromise was unconstitutional because it infringed on slave owners' property rights by prohibiting them from bringing their slaves into the territories. While many Democrats hoped that *Dred Scott* would end the dispute over slavery in the territories, the decision sparked further outrage in the North.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009236–238-111) Lincoln denounced it as the product of a conspiracy of Democrats to support the [Slave Power](/wiki/Slave_Power "Slave Power").[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZarefsky199369–110-112) He argued that the decision was at variance with the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), which stated that "all men are created equal ... with certain unalienable rights", among them "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness".[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaffa2000299–300-113)

### Lincoln–Douglas debates and Cooper Union speech

Further information: [Lincoln–Douglas debates](/wiki/Lincoln%E2%80%93Douglas_debates "Lincoln–Douglas debates") and [Cooper Union speech](/wiki/Cooper_Union_speech "Cooper Union speech")

[![Three-quarters-length photograph of Lincoln](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Abraham_Lincoln_1860.jpg/250px-Abraham_Lincoln_1860.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abraham_Lincoln_1860.jpg)

*Abraham Lincoln*, a portrait by [Mathew Brady](/wiki/Mathew_Brady "Mathew Brady") taken February 27, 1860, the day of Lincoln's [Cooper Union speech](/wiki/Cooper_Union_speech "Cooper Union speech") in New York City.

In 1858, Douglas was up for re-election in the U.S. Senate, and Lincoln hoped to defeat him. Many in the party felt that a former Whig should be nominated in 1858, and Lincoln's 1856 campaigning and support of Trumbull had earned him a favor.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009247–248-114) For the first time, Illinois Republicans held a convention to agree upon a Senate candidate, and Lincoln won the nomination with little opposition.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009247–250-115) Lincoln accepted the nomination with great enthusiasm and zeal. After his nomination he delivered his [House Divided Speech](/wiki/Lincoln%27s_House_Divided_Speech "Lincoln's House Divided Speech"):

> "A house divided against itself cannot stand." I believe this government cannot endure, permanently half *slave* and half *free*. I do not expect the Union to be *dissolved*—I do not expect the house to *fall*—but I *do* expect it will cease to be divided. It will become all one thing, or all the other.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009251-116)

The speech created a stark image of the danger of disunion.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarris200798-117) When informed of Lincoln's nomination, Douglas stated, "[Lincoln] is the strong man of the party ... and if I beat him, my victory will be hardly won."[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009258-118)

The Senate campaign featured [seven debates](/wiki/Lincoln%E2%80%93Douglas_debates "Lincoln–Douglas debates") between Lincoln and Douglas; they had an atmosphere akin to a prizefight and drew thousands.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996214–218Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._487–488-119) Lincoln warned that the Slave Power was threatening the values of republicanism, and he accused Douglas of distorting Jefferson's premise that [all men are created equal](/wiki/All_men_are_created_equal "All men are created equal"). In his [Freeport Doctrine](/wiki/Freeport_Doctrine "Freeport Doctrine"), Douglas argued that, despite the *Dred Scott* decision, which he claimed to support, local settlers, under [popular sovereignty](/wiki/Popular_sovereignty_in_the_United_States "Popular sovereignty in the United States"), should be free to choose whether to allow slavery in their territory. He accused Lincoln of having joined the abolitionists.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._501–504,_527–531-120)

Though the Republican legislative candidates won more popular votes, the Democrats won more seats, and the legislature re-elected Douglas. However, Lincoln's articulation of the issues had given him a national political presence.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarwardine200389–90-121) In the aftermath of the 1858 election, newspapers frequently mentioned Lincoln as a potential Republican presidential candidate. While Lincoln was popular in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), he lacked support in the Northeast and was unsure whether to seek the office.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009291–293-122) In January 1860, Lincoln told a group of political allies that he would accept the presidential nomination if offered and, in the following months, [William O. Stoddard](/wiki/William_O._Stoddard "William O. Stoddard")'s *Central Illinois Gazette*, the *Chicago Press & Tribune*, and other local papers endorsed his candidacy.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009307–308-123)

In February 1860, [Henry Ward Beecher](/wiki/Henry_Ward_Beecher "Henry Ward Beecher") invited Lincoln to address his congregation at [Plymouth Church](/wiki/Plymouth_Church_(Brooklyn) "Plymouth Church (Brooklyn)") in Brooklyn, New York. Lincoln agreed, but the venue shifted to [Cooper Union](/wiki/Cooper_Union "Cooper Union") in Manhattan.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmar2025368-124) In his [speech at Cooper Union](/wiki/Cooper_Union_speech "Cooper Union speech"), which he gave on February 27, Lincoln argued that the [Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States") had little use for popular sovereignty, and that in the [Northwest Ordinance](/wiki/Northwest_Ordinance "Northwest Ordinance") they had restricted slavery in the territories;[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_pp._585-586-125) he insisted that morality required opposition to slavery and rejected any "groping for some middle ground between the right and the wrong".[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJaffa2000473-126) Many in the audience thought he appeared awkward and even ugly.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolzer2004108–111-127) But Lincoln demonstrated intellectual leadership, which brought him into contention for the presidency. Journalist [Noah Brooks](/wiki/Noah_Brooks "Noah Brooks") reported, "No man ever before made such an impression on his first appeal to a New York audience".[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarwardine200397Holzer2004157-128) Historian David Herbert Donald described the speech as "a superb political move for an unannounced presidential aspirant."[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996240-129) In response to an inquiry about his ambitions, Lincoln said, "The taste *is* in my mouth a little".[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996241-130)

### 1860 presidential election

Main article: [1860 United States presidential election](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election")

[![Lincoln being carried by two men (one White, one Black) on a long board.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/The_Rail_Candidate.jpg/250px-The_Rail_Candidate.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Rail_Candidate.jpg)

*The Rail Candidate*, a critical [Currier and Ives](/wiki/Currier_and_Ives "Currier and Ives") illustration, which depicted Lincoln's platform in the [1860 presidential campaign](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election") as being held up by a slave and Horace Greeley

On May 9–10, 1860, the Illinois Republican State Convention was held in [Decatur](/wiki/Decatur,_Illinois "Decatur, Illinois").[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996244-131) Exploiting his embellished frontier legend of clearing land and splitting fence rails, Lincoln's supporters promoted him as "The Rail Candidate".[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996244–245-132) On May 18 at the [Republican National Convention](/wiki/1860_Republican_National_Convention "1860 Republican National Convention") in Chicago, Lincoln won the nomination on the third ballot.[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1) A former Democrat, [Hannibal Hamlin](/wiki/Hannibal_Hamlin "Hannibal Hamlin") of Maine, was nominated for vice president to [balance the ticket](/wiki/Ticket_balance "Ticket balance").[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._625-133)

Throughout the 1850s, Lincoln had doubted the prospects of civil war, and his supporters rejected claims that his election would incite secession.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBorittPinsker200210,_13,_18-134) When Douglas was selected as the candidate of the Northern Democrats, delegates from the Southern slave states elected incumbent Vice President [John C. Breckinridge](/wiki/John_C._Breckinridge "John C. Breckinridge") as their candidate.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996253-135) A group of former Whigs and Know Nothings formed the [Constitutional Union Party](/wiki/Constitutional_Union_Party_(United_States) "Constitutional Union Party (United States)") and nominated [John Bell](/wiki/John_Bell_(Tennessee_politician) "John Bell (Tennessee politician)") of Tennessee. Lincoln and Douglas competed for votes in the North, while Bell and Breckinridge found support primarily in the South.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009247–248-114) A nationwide militaristic Republican youth organization, the [Wide Awakes](/wiki/Wide_Awakes "Wide Awakes"), "turned it into one of the most excited elections in American history" and "triggered massive popular enthusiasm", according to the political historian Jon Grinspan.[[132]](#cite_note-136) People of the Northern states knew the Southern states would vote against Lincoln and rallied supporters for him.[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMurrin2006464-137)

As Douglas and the other candidates campaigned, Lincoln gave no speeches, relying on the enthusiasm of the Republican Party. Republican speakers emphasized Lincoln's childhood poverty to demonstrate the power of "free labor", which allowed a common farm boy to work his way to the top by his own efforts.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996254–256-138) Though he did not make public appearances, many sought to visit and write to Lincoln. In the runup to the election, he took an office in the [Illinois state capitol](/wiki/Old_State_Capitol_State_Historic_Site "Old State Capitol State Historic Site") to deal with the influx of attention. He also hired [John George Nicolay](/wiki/John_George_Nicolay "John George Nicolay") as his personal secretary, who would remain in that role during the presidency.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996251–252Burlingame2008vol._1_pp._650–652-139)

On November 6, 1860, Lincoln was elected as the first Republican president. His victory was entirely due to his support in the North and West. No ballots were cast for him in 10 of the 15 Southern slave states.[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMansch200561Donald1996256-140) Lincoln received 1,866,452 votes, or 39.8 percent of the total in a four-way race, carrying the free Northern states, as well as California and Oregon, and winning the electoral vote decisively.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009350-141)

## Presidency (1861–1865)

Main article: [Presidency of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Presidency_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Presidency of Abraham Lincoln")

### First term

#### Secession and inauguration

Main article: [Presidential transition of Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Presidential_transition_of_Abraham_Lincoln "Presidential transition of Abraham Lincoln")

Further information: [Secession winter](/wiki/Secession_winter "Secession winter") and [Baltimore Plot](/wiki/Baltimore_Plot "Baltimore Plot")

[![A large crowd in front of a large building with many pillars.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Flickr_-_USCapitol_-_Abraham_Lincoln%27s_First_Inauguration.jpg/250px-Flickr_-_USCapitol_-_Abraham_Lincoln%27s_First_Inauguration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flickr_-_USCapitol_-_Abraham_Lincoln%27s_First_Inauguration.jpg)

[Lincoln's first inaugural](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln%27s_first_inaugural_address "Abraham Lincoln's first inaugural address") at the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") on March 4, 1861, with the [Capitol dome](/wiki/United_States_Capitol_dome "United States Capitol dome") above the rotunda still under construction.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/18610304_Affairs_of_the_Nation_-_Abraham_Lincoln_inauguration_-_The_New_York_Times.jpg/330px-18610304_Affairs_of_the_Nation_-_Abraham_Lincoln_inauguration_-_The_New_York_Times.jpg)](/wiki/File:18610304_Affairs_of_the_Nation_-_Abraham_Lincoln_inauguration_-_The_New_York_Times.jpg)

Headlines in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* following Lincoln's first inauguration portended imminent hostilities.

After Lincoln's election, secessionists implemented plans to leave the Union before he took office in March 1861.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdgar1998350-142)[[1]](#cite_note-anb-1) On December 20, 1860, South Carolina adopted an ordinance of secession; by February 1, 1861, Florida, Mississippi, Alabama, Georgia, Louisiana, and Texas had followed.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996267Potter1977-143) Six of these states declared themselves to be a sovereign nation, the [Confederate States of America](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America"), selecting [Jefferson Davis](/wiki/Jefferson_Davis "Jefferson Davis") as its provisional president.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996267White2009369-144) The upper South and border states (Delaware, Maryland, Virginia, North Carolina, Tennessee, Kentucky, Missouri, and Arkansas) initially rejected the secessionist appeal.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009362-145) President Buchanan and President-elect Lincoln refused to recognize the Confederacy, declaring secession illegal.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPotter1977520,_569–570-146) On February 11, 1861, Lincoln gave a particularly emotional [farewell address](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln%27s_farewell_address "Abraham Lincoln's farewell address") upon leaving Springfield for Washington.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._1_p._759-147)

Lincoln and the Republicans rejected the proposed [Crittenden Compromise](/wiki/Crittenden_Compromise "Crittenden Compromise") as contrary to the party's platform of free-soil in the [territories](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States").[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWhite2009360–361-148) Lincoln said, "I will suffer death before I consent ... to any concession or compromise which looks like buying the privilege to take possession of this government to which we have a constitutional right".[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996268-149) Lincoln supported the [Corwin Amendment](/wiki/Corwin_Amendment "Corwin Amendment") to the [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"), which would have protected slavery in states where it already existed. The amendment passed Congress and was awaiting ratification by the required three-fourths of the states when Southern states began to secede.[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVorenberg200122Vile2003280–281-150) On March 4, 1861, in his [first inaugural address](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln%27s_first_inaugural_address "Abraham Lincoln's first inaugural address"), Lincoln said that, because he held "such a provision to now be implied constitutional law, I have no objection to its being made express, and irrevocable".[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurlingame2008vol._2_p._48-151)

[![Cartoon of Lincoln in a kilt and fancy hat doing a jig](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Lincoln_LadyPNG.jpg/250px-Lincoln_LadyPNG.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_LadyPNG.jpg)

Lincoln was mocked by opposition papers falsely claiming that he sneaked into Washington in disguise after the 1860 election.

Due to secessionist plots, careful attention was paid to Lincoln's security and the train he took to Washington. The president-elect evaded suspected [assassins in Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Plot "Baltimore Plot"). He traveled in disguise, wearing a soft felt hat instead of his customary [stovepipe hat](/wiki/Stovepipe_hat "Stovepipe hat") and draping an overcoat over his shoulders while hunching to conceal his height. On February 23, 1861, he arrived in Washington, D.C., which was placed under military guard. Many in the opposition press criticized his secretive journey; opposition newspapers mocked Lincoln with caricatures showing him sneaking into the capital.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996273–279Burlingame2008vol._2_pp._32–39-152) Lincoln directed his inaugural address to the South, proclaiming once again that he had no inclination to abolish slavery in the Southern states:

> Apprehension seems to exist among the people of the Southern States, that by the accession of a Republican Administration, their property, and their peace, and personal security, are to be endangered. There has never been any reasonable cause for such apprehension. Indeed, the most ample evidence to the contrary has all the while existed, and been open to their inspection. It is found in nearly all the published speeches of him who now addresses you. I do but quote from one of those speeches when I declare that "I have no purpose, directly or indirectly, to interfere with the institution of slavery in the States where it exists. I believe I have no lawful right to do so, and I have no inclination to do so."

— [First inaugural address](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln%27s_first_inaugural_address "Abraham Lincoln's first inaugural address"), 4 March 1861[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESandburg2002212Lincoln1953333-153)

The president ended his address with an appeal to the people of the South: "We are not enemies, but friends.... The mystic chords of memory, stretching from every battle-field, and patriot grave, to every living heart and hearthstone ... will yet swell the chorus of the Union, when again touched ... by the better angels of our nature".[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996283–284-154) According to Donald, the failure of the [Peace Conference of 1861](/wiki/Peace_Conference_of_1861 "Peace Conference of 1861") to attract the attendance of seven of the Confederate states signaled that legislative compromise was not a practical expectation.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDonald1996268,_279-155)
